entries.title: DOM Invader overview
entries.body:
# DOM Invader overview

**DOM Invader** is a Burp Suite tool that lives not in the main Burp window but right inside the browser: it is a preinstalled extension within the built-in **Burp's browser**. Its job is to help find vulnerabilities that arise on the client side, in JavaScript that runs in the victim's browser. This is a whole class of bugs that ordinary "server-side" tools (Repeater, Intruder, the HTTP-based scanner) struggle to catch, because the vulnerability is born not in the server's response but in how the client-side code handles data already inside the page.

## Why it is needed

The classic example is **DOM-based XSS**. The server returns completely harmless HTML, but a script on the page takes a value from `location.hash`, `document.referrer`, or from an incoming `postMessage` and, without sanitizing it, stuffs it into a dangerous sink such as `innerHTML` or `eval`. To find such a bug manually you have to: read all the JavaScript, mentally trace the data path from the source to the sink, account for all event handlers, encoding, and filters. On a real application with minified code this is brutally labor-intensive.

DOM Invader automates exactly this "source -> sink" tracing. It instruments (hooks) key DOM sources and DOM sinks right in the browser runtime and shows, in a clear form, which of them are controllable and in what context your input ends up.

## What it can find

DOM Invader covers several related classes of client-side vulnerabilities:

* **DOM XSS** - the main scenario. Using a marker canary (**Canary**), it tracks where your input "flows" and highlights controllable sinks, indicating the context (HTML or JS) and how the input is sanitized.
* **DOM XSS via web messages** - logs, modifies, and resends `postMessage()` messages, and also generates probe messages itself to surface vulnerable handlers.
* **Client-side prototype pollution** - automatically looks for prototype pollution sources (`Object.prototype`) and scans the page for gadgets through which the pollution turns into a real attack.
* **DOM clobbering** - automatically looks for places where HTML injected into the page can "override" (clobber) variables and change the behavior of scripts.

## How this works in the browser

After you enable it (see the section on enabling), DOM Invader adds a **new tab in the browser's DevTools**. Its central part is the so-called **augmented DOM view**. This is not the ordinary element tree from the **Elements** tab but a representation tailored for hunting vulnerabilities: it instantly shows the controllable sinks on the page, the injection context (HTML/JS), and how your input is filtered. In effect, instead of reading the code by hand you immediately see a ready-made list of "where my input flows and what happens to it."

## An important nuance about availability

DOM Invader works **only in the built-in Burp's browser** (the Chromium-based **Burp's browser**). You cannot install it in your system Chrome or Firefox - it is part of Burp, not a separate extension from a store. For this reason, all the client-side bug testing scenarios in ARS3NAL are tied to launching Burp's browser specifically from the **Proxy > Intercept** tab.

## Where it fits into the workflow

DOM Invader does not replace the other tools; it complements them on the "last mile" - where the data has already reached the browser. The typical chain: you get traffic and access to the application through the **Proxy**, you work server-side injections in **Repeater/Intruder**, and as soon as you suspect a client-side bug (input is reflected in the DOM, the page actively uses `postMessage`, the URL fragment holds parameters) you switch to Burp's browser and point DOM Invader at it.

entries.title: Enabling DOM Invader
entries.body:
# Enabling DOM Invader

DOM Invader ships together with **Burp's browser**, but by default it is **off** - so that it does not interfere with sites' operation until you ask it to. It is enabled in a couple of clicks, but there is an important detail: after enabling it you must reload the page.

## Enabling steps

```
1. Откройте вкладку Proxy > Intercept и нажмите "Open Browser"
   (запустится встроенный Burp's browser).
2. В правом верхнем углу окна браузера нажмите на логотип Burp Suite.
   Если логотипа не видно — сначала нажмите иконку-пазл (extensions),
   логотип DOM Invader появится среди расширений.
3. Откроется панель с настройками. Перейдите на вкладку DOM Invader.
4. Переключите тумблер так, чтобы было "DOM Invader is on".
5. Нажмите Reload — без перезагрузки изменения НЕ применятся.
```

The reload is mandatory because DOM Invader works by instrumenting DOM sources and sinks. This instrumentation is installed at the moment the page loads, so an already-open page must be reloaded for the tool to "hook into" its scripts.

## Open the working panel (DevTools)

The DOM Invader interface itself lives in the browser's DevTools:

```
1. Кликните ПКМ в любом месте страницы и выберите Inspect
   (или нажмите F12) — откроется DevTools.
2. Среди вкладок DevTools появится новая вкладка DOM Invader.
3. Совет: пристыкуйте (dock) панель DevTools к НИЖНЕМУ краю окна —
   так расширенный DOM-вид читается удобнее всего.
```

## The Canary

When enabled, DOM Invader immediately sets up a **Canary** - a random marker string (for example, `xh9XKYlV`). This is a "dye" by which the tool tracks the movement of your data across the page: you inject the canary into a source, and DOM Invader watches which sinks it surfaces in. The current canary value is shown in the same DOM Invader settings panel; it can be copied or replaced with your own (see the section on settings).

## Persisting state between sessions

By default, Burp's browser remembers settings and history, including whether DOM Invader was enabled. This is convenient: enable it once, and on the next launch it is active again. But if you need a "clean" browser with no saved state (for example, to rule out the influence of old data, or for privacy when working on someone else's scope), disable the storage option: **Settings > Tools > Burp's browser**, clear **Store settings and history after closing**.

## A quick check that everything works

The simplest way to make sure DOM Invader is active:

```
1. Откройте любую страницу, где ваш ввод попадает в DOM
   (например, поиск, который отражает запрос на странице).
2. В панели DOM Invader (в DevTools) скопируйте канарейку
   или нажмите Inject URL params / Inject forms.
3. Если в расширенном DOM-виде появились записи о приёмниках,
   куда «дотекла» канарейка, — инструмент работает.
```

If the DOM Invader tab is not in DevTools or no records appear, the reason is almost always that you forgot to click **Reload** after toggling the switch on.

entries.title: Finding DOM XSS (Canary, source -> sink)
entries.body:
# Finding DOM XSS with DOM Invader

DOM-based XSS is an XSS that arises entirely on the client side: vulnerable JavaScript takes data from an attacker-controlled **source** and passes it into a dangerous **sink** without proper handling. DOM Invader is built exactly around this "source -> sink" model: it highlights the sinks your input flows into and immediately shows the injection context.

## The main instrument - the Canary

At its core is the **Canary** - a random marker string (visible in the top-left corner of the **DOM** view). The logic is simple:

1. You place the canary into a potential source (URL parameter, form field, hash, web message, and so on).
2. After that, DOM Invader parses the DOM and looks for which sinks the canary surfaced in.
3. The sinks it finds are shown in the **DOM** view, **sorted by "interestingness"** - the most promising for XSS at the top.

## Automatic injection: Inject URL params and Inject forms

Injecting the canary into every parameter by hand is tedious, so there are two accelerator buttons:

* **Inject URL params** - reopens the page, automatically appending the canary to **every** key and value of the query parameters.
* **Inject forms** - automatically inserts the canary into **every** field of the HTML forms on the page.

This is the fastest way to "hit" all the obvious sources at once and instantly see which of them reach sinks.

## What the augmented DOM view shows

For each sink the canary lands in, DOM Invader shows not just the fact that it landed but the context, which is what decides whether the bug is exploitable:

* **the sink type** - whether it is an HTML sink (for example, `innerHTML`) or a JS sink (for example, `eval`, `setTimeout`, `Function`);
* **outer HTML / surrounding** - what is around the canary, i.e. exactly which markup/string you ended up in;
* **frame path** - in which frame of the page this happened (relevant for sites with iframes);
* **event** - which JS event led the sink to fire.

Thanks to this, you immediately see the **XSS context** and understand which characters and events are needed to break out of the current context.

## The Stack Trace column - jump into the code

The output has a **Stack Trace** column. Clicking the link in it takes you straight to the relevant line of client-side code in the browser's **Sources** tab. This is critical when there are conditions/filters between the source and the sink: you see the real data path and can understand what check is on the way and how to bypass it.

## The full workflow

```
1. Включите DOM Invader и откройте уязвимую страницу
   в Burp's browser, откройте DevTools > DOM Invader.
2. Нажмите Inject URL params (и/или Inject forms),
   либо вручную вставьте Canary в подозрительный источник.
3. В DOM-виде посмотрите приёмники, куда дотекла канарейка
   (они отсортированы по интересности — начните с верхних).
4. Раскройте запись: определите контекст (HTML или JS),
   посмотрите surrounding HTML и сработавшее событие.
5. По ссылке в Stack Trace прыгните в Sources и изучите,
   проходит ли ввод через фильтры/кодирование.
6. Подбирайте полезную нагрузку: экспериментируйте с
   спецсимволами (<, >, ", ', `, скобки) и смотрите,
   какие из них НЕ экранируются.
7. Соберите рабочий payload прямо по контексту и проверьте,
   что он вызывает выполнение (например, alert()).
```

## Practical scenarios

* **Searching via the hash.** Many SPAs read `location.hash`. Drop the canary into the fragment: `https://target/#search=xh9XKYlV` and watch whether it surfaces in `innerHTML`. A hash source is especially valuable because the URL fragment does not go to the server - the bug is purely client-side.
* **Separating "reflection" from "exploitability."** The canary may land in a dozen places, but only those where the context allows breaking out are exploitable. The context column and the surrounding HTML help you immediately discard safe landings (for example, the canary landed in a text node and is correctly escaped).
* **Minified code.** When the logic is hidden in a built bundle, **Stack Trace** + jumping to **Sources** save hours: you are immediately on the right line instead of searching through the whole file by hand.
* **Character probing.** If `<` is escaped but a quote is not, you may be inside a JS string or an attribute: then the vector is built not on `<script>` but on breaking out of the string/attribute and attaching an event handler.

## Important

DOM Invader shows **where** the input flows and **in what context** - this sharply narrows the search, but you still assemble the final payload yourself, based on the specific context and filters. The tool is "a highlighting of the path," not an auto-exploit for arbitrary DOM XSS.

entries.title: Testing web messages (postMessage)
entries.body:
# Testing web messages (postMessage)

`postMessage()` is the standard mechanism for safe message exchange between windows/frames of different origins. The problem arises when a `message` handler trusts the incoming data or poorly validates the `origin`: then data from another window can reach a dangerous sink and cause DOM XSS (or another logic vulnerability). DOM Invader can intercept, log, modify, and resend such messages - and it probes the handlers itself.

## Enabling interception

Message interception is enabled with a separate toggle:

```
1. В панели DOM Invader откройте настройки.
2. Включите Postmessage interception.
3. Нажмите Reload, чтобы перехват начал работать.
```

After this, DOM Invader will automatically capture all `postMessage()` calls on the page.

## The Messages view

Intercepted messages are collected in the **Messages** view. For each message you can see its properties and behavior. By default, DOM Invader does not merely listen passively - it **generates and sends its own messages** to all discovered `message`-event handlers in order to actively probe them for vulnerabilities.

## How exactly it probes

To surface weak handlers, DOM Invader modifies messages in two key ways:

* **Canary injection** - it substitutes the **Canary** into the message's `data` property to track whether a controlled value reaches a dangerous sink (the same way as in ordinary DOM XSS).
* **Origin spoofing** - it replaces the message's origin with a fake domain that **fits the expected validation pattern**. This catches broken origin validation: for example, when the code checks "does the origin contain the string `trusted.com`" instead of a strict comparison - then `trusted.com.evil.com` or `eviltrusted.com` will pass the check.

Based on the results, DOM Invader itself assigns each suspicious message a **severity** and a **confidence**, helping you prioritize.

## Manual testing (like a Repeater for messages)

Any message can be examined manually: click a record to see the details of how its properties (`origin`, `data`, `source`) are accessed. The details dialog offers manual "replay":

* edit the **Data** field (substitute your own payload or a modified structure);
* click **Send** to send the modified message again.

In spirit this is the equivalent of Burp Repeater but for web messages: the same "modify -> send -> observe the reaction" cycle, only applied to `postMessage`.

## A ready-made PoC

When a vulnerable message has been found and confirmed, click **Build PoC** - DOM Invader will generate the HTML code of a proof-of-concept exploit (it is copied to the clipboard). This HTML opens the target page and sends it a malicious message - convenient to attach to a bug bounty report as a reproducible proof-of-concept.

## The full workflow

```
1. Включите Postmessage interception и нажмите Reload.
2. Походите по приложению / выполните действия, которые
   инициируют обмен postMessage (виджеты, OAuth-попапы,
   встраиваемые iframe, чаты).
3. Откройте вид Messages — изучите перехваченные сообщения
   и автоматически найденные подозрительные (по severity).
4. Кликните по интересному сообщению, посмотрите, как
   обработчик читает origin/data и куда уходит data.
5. Вручную: отредактируйте Data, нажмите Send, проверьте
   реакцию (например, отрисовался ли HTML, сработал ли sink).
6. Подтвердив уязвимость, нажмите Build PoC и сохраните
   сгенерированный HTML для отчёта.
```

## Practical scenarios

* **Weak origin validation.** The most common bug - a handler accepts a message without checking `origin` at all, or checks it with a substring. The automatic origin spoofing in DOM Invader finds this with no manual fuss.
* **data reaching innerHTML.** If `data` contains HTML/markup that then ends up in a child element's `innerHTML`, that is a direct path to XSS. A canary injected into `data` will immediately reveal such a sink.
* **JSON handlers.** Many handlers expect `JSON.parse(event.data)` with an object. DOM Invader can work with JSON data too - the **Data** field can be edited to the required structure and sent.
* **Pairing with prototype pollution.** Messages carrying JSON are also a source of client-side prototype pollution: DOM Invader checks such `postMessage` calls for prototype pollution as well (see the relevant section).

entries.title: Prototype pollution (client-side)
entries.body:
# Prototype pollution (client-side)

**Client-side prototype pollution** is a vulnerability in which an attacker can add or override a property in `Object.prototype` through specially crafted input (usually with keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, `prototype`). Since almost all objects inherit from `Object.prototype`, the "planted" property surfaces throughout the application. The pollution by itself is only half the job: to get a real attack (for example, XSS) you need a **gadget** - a place in the code that reads this property and passes it into a dangerous sink. DOM Invader automates both phases: finding the source and finding the gadget.

## Enabling

Prototype pollution detection is enabled in the settings, in the **Attack types** section:

```
1. В панели DOM Invader откройте настройки > Attack types.
2. Включите Prototype pollution.
3. Нажмите Reload.
```

After this, in addition to the ordinary DOM XSS sources/sinks, DOM Invader also begins looking for prototype pollution sources.

## Where it looks for sources

DOM Invader automatically checks two typical sources for prototype pollution:

* **URL parameters**, primarily through `location.hash` (and also the query string);
* **JSON objects that arrived via web messages** (`postMessage` with JSON in `data`).

The sources it finds are shown in the **DOM** view, together with a description of **alternative techniques on the same source** (different ways to reach the prototype through the same entry point).

## Confirming the source: the Test button

When a potential source has been found, click **Test**. DOM Invader:

* opens a new tab where it tries, through this source, to add an arbitrary property to `Object.prototype`;
* outputs a proof result to the browser console.

Specifically, as a PoC it creates a property **`testproperty`** with the value **`'DOM_INVADER_PP_POC'`** and verifies that it appeared in the prototype chain. Roughly speaking, on an empty object it runs `console.log(myObject.testproperty);` - if `DOM_INVADER_PP_POC` is printed in the console, then the prototype really is being polluted and the source is confirmed.

## Finding the gadget: the Scan for gadgets button

Finding the source is not enough - you need to turn it into impact. Manually searching the code for gadgets is extremely tedious, so there is **Scan for gadgets**:

```
1. Подтвердив источник, нажмите Scan for gadgets.
2. DOM Invader откроет новую вкладку и запустит авто-скан.
3. Он ищет управляемое (через загрязнение прототипа) свойство,
   которое передаётся в приёмник без должной санитизации.
4. Результаты показываются в DOM-виде — с указанием
   достижимых приёмников (например, innerHTML).
```

## A ready-made exploit: the Exploit button

When a gadget has been found, click **Exploit** - DOM Invader will assemble a working PoC, combining three parts: the pollution **source** + the **gadget** (the controlled property) + the **sink**. The result is a clear proof of impact, for example a successful `alert()` call.

## The full workflow

```
1. Включите Prototype pollution (Attack types) и Reload.
2. Походите по приложению / откройте страницы с параметрами
   в URL-фрагменте и местами, где приходит JSON по postMessage.
3. В DOM-виде найдите обнаруженные источники загрязнения.
4. Нажмите Test — убедитесь, что прототип реально загрязняется
   (в консоли появилось DOM_INVADER_PP_POC).
5. Нажмите Scan for gadgets — дождитесь списка гаджетов
   и достижимых приёмников.
6. Нажмите Exploit — получите готовый PoC и проверьте импакт
   (например, alert()).
```

## Practical scenarios

* **The full cycle in one go.** The chain **Test -> Scan for gadgets -> Exploit** walks the whole path from "a parameter in the hash controls the prototype" to "here's your XSS" with practically no manual coding - this dramatically speeds up bug bounty work.
* **Gadgets in libraries.** Often the gadget lives not in the application's code but in a popular library (templating engines, config builders, jQuery-like `$.extend`). The auto-scan finds them even if you do not know the library's internals.
* **JSON via postMessage.** If the application accepts config/state via `postMessage` as JSON, that is a classic vector: a `__proto__` key inside such JSON pollutes the prototype. DOM Invader checks this source automatically (overlapping with the web messages section).
* **Alternative techniques.** The hint about "alternative techniques on the same source" is useful when a head-on `__proto__` is filtered but a bypass through `constructor.prototype` or another syntax gets through.

## Important

Prototype pollution without a gadget is, as a rule, a low-severity finding. The value of DOM Invader is precisely that it links the source to the gadget and the sink and shows the **real impact**. For a report, always aim to attach the "source -> gadget -> sink" chain that the **Exploit** button gives you, not just the fact of prototype pollution.

entries.title: DOM clobbering
entries.body: # DOM clobbering **DOM clobbering** is a technique in which an attacker injects seemingly harmless HTML into the page (with no `

entries.title: DOM Invader settings
entries.body:
# DOM Invader settings

All DOM Invader settings live in its panel: click the Burp Suite logo in the top-right corner of Burp's browser and go to the **DOM Invader** tab. Below is a breakdown of the main groups of settings and what each one affects. The general rule: after changing most settings you need to click **Reload**, otherwise they will not apply (the instrumentation is installed when the page loads).

## The main toggle

* **DOM Invader on/off** - the global enable/disable for the tool. Off by default.

## The Canary

The canary is a marker string used to track the movement of your data across the page. Controls:

* the **current value** of the canary is shown at the bottom of the panel (by default a random string such as `xh9XKYlV`);
* **Copy** - copy the canary to the clipboard (for manual injection into sources);
* an input field + **Randomize** - set your own string or generate a new random one;
* **Update canary** - apply the new value;
* then **Reload**, which is mandatory.

An important rule: the chosen canary **must not naturally occur on the page**, otherwise there will be false positives (DOM Invader will decide that "your" marker reached a sink when it is just the site's text).

## Attack types

These determine what exactly DOM Invader looks for beyond basic DOM XSS:

* **by default** - ordinary DOM XSS sources and sinks;
* **Prototype pollution** - additionally look for client-side prototype pollution sources (there is also a settings icon for fine-tuning detection behavior);
* **DOM clobbering** - additionally look for DOM clobbering vulnerabilities (off by default so as not to interfere with the site's operation).

## Web messages

* **Postmessage interception** - enables interception, logging, and auto-probing of `postMessage()` messages. Without it the **Messages** view is not populated.

## Other settings (Misc)

A group of pinpoint options affecting convenience and interception behavior:

* **Message filtering by stack trace** - compares the stack traces of records and hides duplicates that point to the same line of code already in the list. Removes the "noise" of repeating identical events.
* **Auto-fire events** - automatically triggers `click` and `mouseover` events on every element as soon as the page loads. Helps provoke handlers that would otherwise fire only on a user action - that is, it helps find sinks hidden behind events.
* **Redirection prevention** - blocks client-side redirects so you stay on the same page. Very useful when vulnerable code fires but immediately whisks you away with a redirect, leaving no chance to examine the result.
* **Add breakpoint before redirect** - sets a breakpoint right before the code that triggers the redirect (to debug the cause of the navigation in DevTools).
* **Inject canary into all sources** - automatically injects the canary into all discovered sources on the page (with configurable parameters). Saves manual clicks on **Inject URL params/forms**.
* **Configuring callbacks** - lets you run your own functions when DOM Invader finds a source, a sink, or a web message (advanced automation for your own scenarios).
* **Remove Permissions-Policy header** - automatically strips the `Permissions-Policy` header from responses when it is present. Needed because this policy can restrict behavior that interferes with testing.

## Persisting settings between sessions

This is a setting not of DOM Invader itself but of Burp's browser, though it directly affects it: **Settings > Tools > Burp's browser > Store settings and history after closing**. If enabled, DOM Invader will remember that it was active, along with its configuration. If you need a clean start with no saved state, disable it.

## Practical recommendations

* **The basic set for DOM XSS.** It is enough to enable DOM Invader and use **Inject URL params/forms** + the canary. Everything else is as needed.
* **Enable Redirection prevention when you "can't catch sight of" the bug.** If the page instantly redirects after a sink fires, without this option you simply will not be able to see the result.
* **Auto-fire events against "hidden" sinks.** If input is clearly reflected but a sink is not highlighted, it may be behind an event handler; enable the auto events.
* **Use your own canary on "noisy" sites.** If the default marker accidentally coincides with page content (false positives), set a deliberately unique string via **Update canary**.
* **Enable Prototype pollution / DOM clobbering selectively.** This is not an "always on" thing - add them when there is a reason (JSON via postMessage and parameters in the hash - for PP; allowed but filtered HTML - for clobbering). On a live site, enable DOM clobbering consciously because of its possible impact on the page's operation.
* **Remember Reload.** The most common cause of "it doesn't work" is changing the toggle/canary and forgetting to reload the page.
